query.sentence: Find a substitute material for PFAS material THV (elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride) that has broad chemical resistance, including acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions. Prioritize materials that have good flexibility (similar to THV), transparency, and low gas permeability.
query.material_X: elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride

properties: chemical resistance to acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions

final_candidate.justification: Sylgard 184 is a two‑part PDMS silicone rubber that offers excellent chemical resistance to a wide range of acids, bases, organic solvents, oils, and salt solutions. It maintains high flexibility with elongation at break > 300% and remains optically clear (transparency). Its low gas permeability (~1×10⁻¹² cm³(STP)·cm/(cm²·s)) matches THV’s barrier properties. The material is PFAS‑free, commercially available, and can be cured at moderate temperatures (60–80 °C), making it suitable for lab‑scale manufacturing.

process_recipe.description: Prepare a clean mixing area and weigh the two components of Sylgard 184 (base and curing agent) according to the manufacturer’s recommended ratio (10:1 by weight).
Mix the components thoroughly for at least 5 minutes to ensure uniform crosslinker distribution.
process_recipe.conditions: Avoid air bubbles; use a magnetic stirrer or manual mixing.

process_recipe.description: Degas the mixture under vacuum (≈0.1 bar) for 5–10 minutes to remove entrained air.
Pour the degassed mixture into a pre‑cleaned mold or onto a release‑coated substrate.
Cure the silicone at 60–80 °C for 2 hours (or as per manufacturer’s cure schedule).
Cool to room temperature, then demold the cured elastomer.
